perturbation.original_text: End User is the final purchaser or licensee who has acquired Products for its own internal use and not for resale, remarketing or redistribution. An entity that performs stocking, sparing or warehousing activities for third parties or procures Cisco Products, Services or Software for delivery to third parties is not an End User.
perturbation.changed_text: End User is the final purchaser or licensee who has acquired Products for any use, including resale, remarketing or redistribution. An entity that performs stocking, sparing or warehousing activities for third parties or procures Cisco Products, Services or Software for delivery to third parties is an End User.
perturbation.explanation: By changing the definition of 'End User' to include those who acquire products for resale, remarketing, or redistribution, the contract contradicts standard interpretations of 'end user' which typically excludes resellers. This introduces ambiguity regarding the rights and obligations of parties further down the distribution chain, potentially affecting warranty claims and support responsibilities.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UCC defines 'buyer' in a commercial context. By redefining 'End User' to include resellers, the contract creates confusion about whether downstream purchasers are afforded protections under the UCC, such as implied warranties of merchantability or fitness for a particular purpose. The original text aligns with standard interpretations excluding resellers; the modified text does not.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-103. Definitions and Index of Definitions. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-103. Definitions and Index of Definitions. (1) In this Article unless the context otherwise requires (a) " Buyer " means a person who buys or contracts to buy goods . (b) " Good faith " in the case of a merchant means honesty in fact and the observance of reasonable commercial standards of fair dealing in the trade. (c) " Receipt " of goods means taking physical possession of them. (d) " Seller " means a person who sells or contracts to sell goods . (2) Other definitions applying to this Article or to specified Parts thereof, and the sections in which they appear are: "Acceptance". Section 2-606. "Banker's credit". Section 2-325. "Between Merchants". Section 2-104. "Cancellation". Section 2-106(4). "Commercial unit". Section 2-105. "Confirmed credit". Section 2-325. "Conforming to contract". Section 2-106. "Contract for sale". Section 2-106. "Cover". Section 2-712. "Entrusting". Section 2-403. "Financing agency". Section 2-104. "Future Goods". Section 2-105. "Goods". Section 2-105. "Identification". Section 2-501. "Installment contract". Section 2-612. "Letter of Credit". Section 2-325. "Lot". Section 2-105. "Merchant". Section 2-104. "Overseas". Section 2-323. "Person in position of Seller". Section 2-707. "Present sale". Section 2-106. "Sale". Section 2-106. "Sale on approval". Section 2-326. "Sale or return". Section 2-326. "Termination". Section 2-106. (3) The following definitions in other Articles apply to this Article: "Check". Section 3-104. "Consignee". Section 7-102. "Consignor". Section 7-102. "Consumer Goods". Section 9-109. "Dishonor". Section 3-507. "Draft". Section 3-104. (4) In addition Article 1 contains general definitions and principles of construction and interpretation applicable throughout this Article. perturbation.justification: The modification to the 'End User' definition directly contradicts the standard interpretation of the term in commercial law, which typically excludes those who acquire products for resale. By including resellers as 'End Users,' the revised contract introduces ambiguity regarding the protections and obligations of downstream purchasers under the UCC, as explained in the Law Explanation. Snippet 1, defining 'Buyer' as someone who 'buys or contracts to buy goods,' when read in conjunction with the changed definition, creates confusion about whether these downstream 'buyers' are afforded UCC protections, such as implied warranties.

perturbation.original_text: Distributor agrees not to solicit orders for Products or Services, or to engage salespeople or establish warehouses or other distribution centers for the redistribution of Products or Services, outside the Territory, except to the extent advertising is placed in a particular advertising medium (except catalogs) which is distributed both inside and outside of the Territory.
perturbation.changed_text: Distributor is free to solicit orders for Products or Services, and to engage salespeople or establish warehouses or other distribution centers for the redistribution of Products or Services, outside the Territory. This right cannot be abridged.
perturbation.explanation: By granting the Distributor the unrestricted right to solicit orders and establish distribution centers outside the designated territory, this modification fundamentally alters the territorial restrictions outlined in the original agreement. This creates a conflict of interest and introduces uncertainty regarding enforcement of the territorial limitations.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly a violation, removing territorial restrictions could potentially facilitate anti-competitive behavior if it leads to collusion or market allocation agreements with other distributors, violating the Sherman Antitrust Act. The original clause sought to prevent such scenarios by setting clear territorial boundaries.

Trusts, etc., in restraint of trade illegal; penalty Every contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations, is declared to be illegal. Every person who shall make any contract or engage in any combination or conspiracy hereby declared to be illegal shall be
perturbation.justification: The modification directly contradicts the original agreement by explicitly allowing the distributor to solicit orders and establish distribution centers outside the designated territory. While the LLM's Law Explanation states that this 'could potentially facilitate anti-competitive behavior', the scraped law snippet from 15 USC 1 states that 'Every contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations, is declared to be illegal'. By removing territorial restrictions, the modified contract opens the door to activities that could be seen as a 'combination...in restraint of trade', and directly contradicts the original intent of the agreement. This aligns with the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction' since the changed text uses terms that lead to non-compliance.

perturbation.original_text: Distributor will pass through to Resellers all written limited warranties provided by Cisco with Products purchased by Distributor and Distributor shall contractually require (in whatever manner Distributor deems appropriate) each such Reseller to provide such warranty and other terms to any End User to which the Reseller redistributes the Product.
perturbation.changed_text: Distributor may choose to provide resellers with limited warranties for cisco products, and may choose the warranty to give to any End User to which the Reseller redistributes the Product.
perturbation.explanation: By making the passing through of warranties optional, the modified text allows the Distributor to circumvent the original intent of providing End Users with the same warranty coverage offered by Cisco. This creates a legal contradiction because it removes a safeguard designed to protect consumers and introduces ambiguity regarding the applicable warranty terms.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the Magnuson-Moss Warranty Act primarily governs consumer product warranties, making the warranty pass-through optional could allow the distributor to disclaim or modify implied warranties in a way that disadvantages the end-user. This could lead to issues with compliance, particularly if the distributor's altered warranty doesn't meet the minimum standards for a 'full' warranty as defined by the Act. The original wording ensures end users receive the same warranty originally intended.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates that the distributor pass through all warranties to resellers, ensuring that the end-user receives the same warranty coverage offered by Cisco. The changed text makes this warranty pass-through optional. The Perturbation Explanation states that this change 'removes a safeguard designed to protect consumers and introduces ambiguity regarding the applicable warranty terms.' Since the 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction' arises when contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract unenforceable, and the warranty term is clearly altered to favor the distributor, this creates a contradiction. While the law snippet is missing, the original text mandates a practice that is made optional in the changed text, causing the distributor to no longer be obligated to guarantee End Users with the same warranty originally intended.
